title: Adjoints of Matroids
overall_summary: The paper studies adjoints of finite matroids, where an adjoint of M is a matroid adM whose flat lattice contains an order-reversed copy of the flat lattice of M, sending hyperplanes of M to points of adM. Its most relevant results are that adjoints preserve and reflect connectivity, that modular matroids have a unique adjoint up to isomorphism and this adjoint is exactly the matroid determined by the opposite lattice L(M)^op, and that adjoint sequences either terminate, cycle through finite projective geometries, or converge to infinite projective geometries. The core techniques are to relabel an adjoint by the hyperplanes of M, encode elements of M as hyperplanes H[e] of the adjoint, compare adjoints with the extension lattice of M via linear subclasses of hyperplanes, and use modular-geometric lattice intersection properties. For the original problem, the key takeaway is that an order-reversing self-duality of the flat lattice is an adjoint-like polarity phenomenon; in the simple matroid setting, the paper’s results make such phenomena highly rigid and closely tied to modular/projective-geometric structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.2 and formula (2.1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite matroid with flat lattice L(M), hyperplane set H(M), and rank r(M). A matroid adM is called an adjoint of M if r(adM)=r(M) and there is an injective order-reversing map φ:L(M)→L(adM) that sends the coatoms of L(M), equivalently the hyperplanes of M, bijectively onto the atoms of L(adM), equivalently the elements of the simple matroid adM. For every flat X of M, the adjoint map satisfies φ(X)=∨{φ(H): H∈H(M), X⊆H}=∪{φ(H): H∈H(M), X⊆H}, where the join and union are taken in L(adM).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the paper’s defining setup for adjoints. The displayed formula is recorded as a basic consequence of the definition: the flat X is below exactly the hyperplanes H containing X, and under the order-reversing embedding those hyperplanes become atoms of adM whose join is the flat φ(X). The equality with union uses the fact that in a simple matroid, the join of a set of atoms is the closure of their union, and in the adjoint-map image this is denoted by the union of those atoms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid, let adM be an adjoint of M, and let φ:L(M)→L(adM) be an adjoint map. For flats X,Y∈L(M): (i) if X covers Y in L(M), then φ(Y) covers φ(X) in L(adM); (ii) r_adM(φ(X))=r(M)-r_M(X); (iii) φ(X)∧φ(Y)=φ(X∨Y), where ∧ and ∨ are the meet and join in the indicated flat lattices; (iv) r_adM(φ(X))+r_adM(φ(Y))=r_adM(φ(X)∧φ(Y))+r_adM(φ(X)∨φ(Y)); and (v) if H1,...,Hm are hyperplanes of M such that H1∩...∩Hm ⫋ H1∩...∩H_{m-1} ⫋ ... ⫋ H1∩H2 ⫋ H1, then φ(H1),...,φ(Hm) is an independent set of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites these properties from Bixby-Coullard and Hochstättler-Kromberg rather than reproving them. The properties are used as structural rules for adjoint maps: cover reversal gives the rank formula by comparing maximal chains; order reversal turns joins in L(M) into meets of images; the stated rank equality is the modular rank equality inside the image sublattice; and the strict chain of intersections of hyperplanes gives successive rank increases for the corresponding atoms in adM, hence independence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fact 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let adM be an adjoint of M. If e is either a loop of M or an element parallel to another element of M, then adM is also an adjoint of the deletion M\e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as an immediate fact. The point is that loops and parallel elements do not affect the simple geometric-lattice data that the adjoint sees: adjoints are simple, and the relevant incidence of flats and hyperplanes is unchanged after deleting a loop or one element from a parallel class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and adM be matroids of the same rank r. Then adM is an adjoint of M if and only if, after relabeling the ground set of adM as E(adM)=H(M), the set H[e]:={H∈H(M): e∈H} is a hyperplane of adM for every nonloop element e∈E(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Fact 2.4 the proof reduces to the case where M is simple. If adM is an adjoint, relabel each atom φ(H) of adM by the hyperplane H of M. For an element e of M, formula (2.1) gives φ(e)=∪_{H∋e}φ(H), which becomes the subset H[e] of H(M). By Proposition 2.3(ii), r_adM(φ(e))=r(M)-1, so H[e] is a hyperplane of adM. Conversely, assume E(adM)=H(M) and all H[e] are hyperplanes of adM. Define ψ:L(M)→L(adM) by ψ(X)=∩_{e∈X}H[e]={H∈H(M): X⊆H}. This is a flat of adM because it is an intersection of hyperplanes. The map is order-reversing: if X⊆Y, then ψ(Y)⊆ψ(X). It sends a hyperplane H0 of M to the singleton {H0}, so it bijects coatoms of L(M) with atoms of L(adM). Finally, ψ is injective because distinct flats of M have distinct collections of containing hyperplanes. Thus ψ is an adjoint map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank r and let adM be an adjoint whose ground set is relabeled as H(M), as in Proposition 3.1. If B is a basis of M and H(e;B) denotes the fundamental hyperplane of e∈B with respect to B, namely the unique hyperplane containing B−{e}, then {H(e;B): e∈B} is a basis of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write B={e1,...,er}. For each k, the fundamental hyperplane H(ei;B) contains ek for all i≠k and does not contain ek when i=k. Therefore the intersections H(e1;B)∩...∩H(ei;B) form a strictly decreasing chain as i increases. Proposition 2.3(v) applied to these hyperplanes implies that the corresponding r elements of adM are independent. Since r(adM)=r, this independent set is a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a loopless matroid and let adM be an adjoint of M. If adM decomposes as a direct sum adM=N1⊕N2, then there are submatroids M1 and M2 of M such that M=M1⊕M2 and Ni is an adjoint of Mi for i=1,2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Fact 2.4, assume M is simple and use the hyperplane labeling E(adM)=H(M) from Proposition 3.1. Since adM=N1⊕N2, every cocircuit of adM lies in one summand, and hyperplanes of adM are partitioned according to which summand ground set they contain. Let H_E(Ni) be the hyperplanes of adM containing E(Ni). Define Ei={e∈E(M): H[e]∈H_E(Ni)}. Then E(M)=E1⊔E2, and neither Ei is empty: if, for instance, E1 were empty, then every H[e] would contain E(N2), forcing every hyperplane of M corresponding to an element of E(N2) to contain all elements of M, impossible. Choose a basis B of M, write B∩E1={e1,...,ek} and B∩E2={e_{k+1},...,er}. By the definition of Ei, every hyperplane of M belonging to E(N1) contains B∩E1, and every hyperplane belonging to E(N2) contains B∩E2. Thus the fundamental hyperplanes H(ei;B) for ei∈E1 lie in E(N2), while those for ei∈E2 lie in E(N1). Lemma 3.2 says all these fundamental hyperplanes form a basis of adM, so r_adM(E(N1))=|B∩E2| and r_adM(E(N2))=|B∩E1|. Repeating this argument with a basis adapted to E1 or E2 shows r_M(E1)=|B∩E1| and r_M(E2)=|B∩E2|; hence r_M(E1)+r_M(E2)=r(M), so M is the direct sum of its restrictions to E1 and E2. Finally, intervals in L(M) corresponding to the two direct-sum factors are carried by the adjoint map onto the flat lattices of N1 and N2, giving Ni=adMi.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4 and Corollaries 3.5-3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a loopless matroid and adM an adjoint of M. If M=M1⊕M2, then adM=N1⊕N2 for submatroids N1,N2 of adM, where Ni is an adjoint of Mi for i=1,2. Consequently, for a loopless matroid M with an adjoint adM, M is connected if and only if adM is connected. Also, if M is loopless and decomposes as the direct sum of its connected components M1,...,Mn, then M has an adjoint if and only if every component Mi has an adjoint.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The direct-sum assertion is cited from Bixby-Coullard. Combined with Theorem 3.3, it gives the equivalence of connectivity for M and any adjoint adM. For the componentwise existence statement, one direction follows by repeatedly applying Theorem 3.3 to decompose an adjoint along connected components. Conversely, if each Mi has an adjoint adMi with adjoint map φi, then adM:=adM1⊕...⊕adMn has rank sum equal to r(M). Every flat X of M is a disjoint union X=⊔Xi with Xi∈L(Mi), and the map φ(X)=⊔φi(Xi) is injective, order-reversing, and sends hyperplanes of M bijectively to atoms of adM. Thus adM is an adjoint of M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. Then M is modular, meaning every pair of flats X,Y satisfies r_M(X∨Y)+r_M(X∧Y)=r_M(X)+r_M(Y), if and only if M has an adjoint adM such that L(adM) is isomorphic to the opposite lattice L(M)^op.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If M is modular, then the opposite lattice L(M)^op is again a geometric lattice; hence it is the flat lattice of a simple matroid, and the natural order-reversing identification L(M)→L(M)^op gives an adjoint of M. Conversely, if M has an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op, then L(M)^op is a geometric lattice. The paper uses the standard lattice fact that a finite geometric lattice has geometric opposite exactly in the modular case. Therefore M is modular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple modular matroid of rank r. Let E(M) denote the extension lattice of M, whose elements are linear subclasses ℋ⊆H(M), ordered by inclusion, where ℋ is linear if whenever H1,H2∈ℋ with r_M(H1∩H2)=r−2, every hyperplane H3 containing H1∩H2 also lies in ℋ. Then the map λ:L(M)^op→E(M) defined by λ(X)=H_X(M):={H∈H(M): X⊆H} is an isomorphism of posets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First λ is well defined. If r_M(X)=r or r−1 this is immediate. If r_M(X)≤r−2 and H1,H2 are distinct hyperplanes containing X, modularity gives r_M(H1∩H2)=r−2; any hyperplane containing H1∩H2 also contains X, so H_X(M) is linear. The map is order-preserving as a map out of L(M)^op: X≤_{op}Y means Y⊆X, hence H_X(M)⊆H_Y(M). It is injective because distinct flats are separated by their sets of containing hyperplanes. For surjectivity, let ℋ be a linear subclass and put X_ℋ=∩_{H∈ℋ}H. Clearly ℋ⊆H_{X_ℋ}(M). To prove the reverse inclusion, suppose H0∈H_{X_ℋ}(M)−ℋ. Linearity implies that no intersection H∩H' with H,H'∈ℋ can be contained in H0, otherwise H0 would be forced into ℋ. Choose H1,...,H_{r−k} in ℋ, where k=r_M(X_ℋ), with a strictly descending chain X_ℋ=∩_{j=1}^{r−k}Hj ⋖ ... ⋖ H1∩H2 ⋖ H1. Let i0 be minimal such that ∩_{j=1}^{i0−1}Hj is not contained in H0 but ∩_{j=1}^{i0}Hj is contained in H0; then i0≥3. The opposite lattice of a modular geometric lattice has the intersection property: for distinct atoms and an element satisfying the appropriate incidence condition, there is another atom below the relevant meet. Applying this in L(M)^op produces a hyperplane H_{0i0} with H_{i0}∩H0⊆H_{0i0} and ∩_{j=1}^{i0−1}Hj⊆H_{0i0}. If H_{0i0} belonged to ℋ, then linearity applied to H_{i0} and H_{0i0} would force H0∈ℋ, contradiction. Thus H_{0i0} is another member of H_{X_ℋ}(M)−ℋ, distinct from H0, and the same construction yields a smaller index. Iterating gives an infinite descent in a finite interval of possible indices, impossible. Hence H_{X_ℋ}(M)=ℋ, proving surjectivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple modular matroid. Then M has exactly one adjoint up to isomorphism, and for every adjoint adM of M one has L(adM)≅L(M)^op.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 4.1 gives existence of an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op. For uniqueness, the paper uses a known characterization due to Cheung: if adM is an adjoint of M, then L(adM) can be viewed as a sublattice of the extension lattice E(M), and L(M)^op embeds as a sublattice of L(adM). Lemma 4.2 identifies L(M)^op with E(M) when M is modular. Thus L(M)^op⊆L(adM)⊆E(M) collapses to equality up to isomorphism. Therefore every adjoint has flat lattice L(M)^op.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. Then M is modular if and only if every connected component of M is either the one-element free matroid U_{1,1} or a finite projective geometry.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this classical characterization from Birkhoff and Oxley. It is used to translate modularity of a simple matroid into projective-geometric connected components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.5 and Theorem 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If (P,L,ι) is a finite projective geometry, its dual geometry is obtained by taking the coatoms of the subspace lattice L(P) as points and declaring two such points incident with a line when their intersection has rank r−2. The lattice of the dual geometry is isomorphic to L(P)^op, and the dual geometry is again a finite projective geometry. Moreover, by the Coordinatization Theorem, every projective geometry of rank r≥4 is isomorphic to PG(r−1,F) for some field F; in particular, every finite projective geometry of rank r≥4 is isomorphic to PG(r−1,q) for a finite field GF(q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 4.5, the paper treats this as a direct consequence of the standard point-hyperplane duality of projective geometries and Proposition 4.1: the subspaces of the dual geometry are the order-reversed subspaces of the original geometry. The Coordinatization Theorem is cited as the Veblen-Young theorem; no proof is given in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid with no connected component of rank 3. Then M is modular if and only if M has an adjoint adM such that adM≅M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose first that M has an adjoint adM isomorphic to M. Since adM and M have the same number of flats and the adjoint map embeds L(M)^op into L(adM), the embedding is onto, so L(adM)≅L(M)^op. Hence L(M)^op is geometric and M is modular by Proposition 4.1. Conversely, assume M is modular. By the direct-sum results of Section 3 it suffices to treat connected components. A connected simple modular component of rank 1 is U_{1,1}, and of rank 2 is U_{2,m}; both are self-adjoint by direct inspection. Since rank 3 components are excluded, any remaining connected component has rank r≥4 and, by Proposition 4.4 and the Coordinatization Theorem, is PG(r−1,q). The unique adjoint is the type I adjoint, and elementary linear algebra gives a bijection between hyperplanes of GF(q)^r and their one-dimensional normal complements. This identifies the type I adjoint of PG(r−1,q) with PG(r−1,q) itself. Combining components gives adM≅M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. Then M is modular if and only if M has an adjoint adM such that |E(M)|=|E(adM)|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes Greene’s theorem that a simple matroid M is modular if and only if the number of hyperplanes of M equals the number of elements of M. In any adjoint, atoms of adM correspond bijectively to hyperplanes of M, so |E(adM)|=|H(M)|. Thus the equality |E(M)|=|E(adM)| is equivalent to |E(M)|=|H(M)|, and hence to modularity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r≤2 and size m. For every integer k≥0, the kth adjoint ad^k M exists. If r=1 then ad^k M≅U_{1,1} for all k, and if r=2 then ad^k M≅U_{2,m} for all k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this follows easily from the fact that matroids of rank at most three always admit adjoints and from direct inspection of rank-one and rank-two flat lattices. A simple connected rank-one matroid is U_{1,1}; a simple connected rank-two matroid of size m is U_{2,m}; their adjoints have the same flat-lattice form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. If M has a second adjoint ad^2 M, then M is isomorphic to a submatroid of ad^2 M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let φ1:L(M)→L(adM) and φ2:L(adM)→L(ad^2 M) be adjoint maps. Their composition is order-preserving on flat lattices and sends each element e of M first to the hyperplane φ1(e) of adM and then to an element φ2(φ1(e)) of ad^2 M. If {e1,...,ek} is independent in M, then Proposition 2.3(ii) and (iii) imply that the intersections ∩_{j=1}^i φ1(ej) have ranks r(M)−i, hence form a strictly decreasing chain. Proposition 2.3(v), applied to the adjoint map φ2, then implies that {φ2φ1(e1),...,φ2φ1(ek)} is independent in ad^2 M. Thus the element map induced by φ2φ1 embeds M as a submatroid of ad^2 M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. If M has a kth adjoint ad^k M for some k≥3 and M≅ad^k M, then M≅adM or M≅ad^2 M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If k is even, repeated use of Lemma 5.2 shows that ad^2 M is a submatroid of ad^k M, and the same embeddings give |E(M)|≤|E(ad^2 M)|≤|E(ad^k M)|. Since ad^k M≅M, all inequalities are equalities, forcing M≅ad^2 M. If k is odd, the same argument applied to the adjoint sequence starting at adM gives M≅adM.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r≥3. Suppose M has an adjoint sequence ad^0 M=M, adM, ad^2 M, ... and that ad^i M≅ad^j M for some nonnegative integers i<j. If r=3, then all later adjoints ad^k M exist for k≥i; if j−i is odd, all ad^k M for k≥i are isomorphic to one fixed finite projective plane, while if j−i is even, the subsequence ad^{i+2k} M is constant up to isomorphism and the subsequence ad^{i+2k+1} M is constant up to isomorphism, each being a finite projective plane. If r≥4, then all later adjoints ad^k M for k≥i are isomorphic to one fixed finite projective geometry PG(r−1,q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 5.3, the repetition ad^i M≅ad^j M reduces to either ad^i M≅ad^{i+1} M when j−i is odd or ad^i M≅ad^{i+2} M when j−i is even. In either case the adjoint relation identifies L(ad^{i+1} M) with L(ad^i M)^op, so ad^i M is modular. Theorem 4.3 then gives uniqueness of adjoints from that stage onward. In rank 3, Proposition 4.4 identifies connected modular simple matroids with finite projective planes, and uniqueness yields the stated constant or two-periodic behavior. In rank at least 4, Theorem 4.8 and Remark 4.7 identify the modular connected matroid and all its adjoints with the same finite projective geometry PG(r−1,q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.6 and Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r with an infinite adjoint sequence ad^0 M, adM, ad^2 M, ... . For i≤j, Lemma 5.2 gives natural embeddings ad^{2i} M→ad^{2j} M, forming a directed system. Let E∞ be the union of the ground sets of all even adjoints, identify elements that become equal under later embeddings, and call the quotient ground set Ebar. A finite subset of Ebar is independent if its representatives become independent in some common even adjoint. Then the resulting matroid Mbar=(Ebar,Ibar) is simple, connected, has rank r, and is the direct limit lim_i ad^{2i} M in the category of matroids with embeddings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The independence axioms for Mbar are checked by moving any finite collection of representatives to a sufficiently late even adjoint, where the ordinary matroid axioms hold. Rank is r because all even adjoints have rank r, and a basis of M maps to an independent r-set. Simplicity follows from simplicity of all adjoints. For connectivity, any two elements of Mbar have representatives in some common even adjoint; that adjoint is connected by Corollary 3.5, so it contains a circuit through them, whose image remains a circuit in Mbar. For the universal property, the canonical maps ad^{2i} M→Mbar are embeddings compatible with the directed system. Any compatible family of embeddings into another matroid N defines a unique map on equivalence classes; compatibility makes it well defined, and independence is preserved by checking in a common finite stage.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.8 and Theorem 5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r≥3 with an infinite adjoint sequence. Then the direct limit lim_i ad^{2i} M of the even adjoints is a projective geometry; the analogous direct limit of the odd adjoints is also a projective geometry. If, in addition, ad^i M is not isomorphic to ad^j M for all 0≤i<j, then for r=3 these two direct limits are infinite projective planes, and for r≥4 both direct limits are isomorphic to the same infinite projective geometry PG(r−1,F) over some infinite field F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 5.8 is stated as a straightforward consequence of the directed-limit construction and is said to be implicit in an exercise from Oriented Matroids; the paper omits the details. For Theorem 5.9, when r≥4, Proposition 5.8 and the Coordinatization Theorem identify the even direct limit with PG(r−1,F) for some field F. If F were finite, PG(r−1,F) would have only finitely many submatroids up to isomorphism, while all even adjoints embed into it; this would force repetitions, contrary to hypothesis. Hence F is infinite. The even adjoints are representable over F, and a cited result of Bixby-Coullard says adjoints of representable matroids are type I adjoints over the same field up to isomorphism; this gives representability of the odd adjoints over F and identifies the odd direct limit with the same PG(r−1,F). For r=3, Proposition 5.8 gives projective planes; finiteness would again force repetitions, so they are infinite.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank greater than three. Then M is representable over some field if and only if M has an infinite adjoint sequence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If M is representable, the type I adjoint construction can be iterated indefinitely, giving an infinite adjoint sequence. Conversely, if M has an infinite adjoint sequence, Proposition 5.8 embeds M into the projective-geometry direct limit of the even adjoints. Since the rank is greater than three, the Coordinatization Theorem realizes that projective geometry as PG(r−1,F) over a field F, so M is representable over F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,A) be an F-represented matroid of rank r on ground set E={e1,...,em}, with A∈F^{r×m}. Let A'∈F^{(m−r)×m} be a matrix whose rows form a basis of the solution space of Ax=0, so A' represents the dual matroid M*. For each circuit C of M let c_C∈F^m be a circuit vector supported exactly on C, and define the Oxley-Wang derived matroid δ_OW M=M[c_C: C∈C(M)]. For each hyperplane H of M*, let h_H be a normal vector to the span of the columns of A' indexed by H, and define the type I adjoint σM*=M[h_H: H∈H(M*)]. Then δ_OW M≅σM*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A circuit C of M corresponds to the hyperplane C*=E−C of M*. Since c_C∈ker A, it can be written uniquely as c_C=hA' for some row vector h. The entries of c_C vanish exactly on C*, so h annihilates precisely the columns of A' indexed by the hyperplane C*; hence h may be chosen as the normal vector h_{C*}. For any circuits C1,...,Ck, the matrix with rows c_{Ci} equals the matrix with rows h_{C_i*} multiplied by A'. Since A' has full row rank, multiplication by A' preserves the row rank of these h-vectors. Thus every set of circuit vectors has the same rank as the corresponding set of normal vectors, proving the two represented matroids are isomorphic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and adM be matroids of the same rank r, and let M* be the dual matroid of M. Then adM is an adjoint of M if and only if its ground set can be relabeled as E(adM)=C(M*), the set of circuits of the dual matroid, equivalently cocircuits of M, in such a way that for every nonloop e∈E(M), the set Cstar[e]:={C*∈C(M*): e∉C*} is a hyperplane of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a dual reformulation of Proposition 3.1. Circuits C* of M* are cocircuits of M, and each corresponds to the hyperplane H=E(M)−C* of M. Under this correspondence, the condition e∉C* is exactly e∈H. Therefore the set Cstar[e] corresponds to H[e]={H∈H(M): e∈H}. Replacing each cocircuit label C* by its complementary hyperplane H turns the stated condition into the hyperplane-incidence condition of Proposition 3.1, and the result follows in both directions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank r and let adM be an adjoint of M whose ground set is relabeled as C(M*), the cocircuits of M. If B is a basis of M and C*(e;B) denotes the fundamental cocircuit of e∈B with respect to B, then {C*(e;B): e∈B} is a basis of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The set has r elements, so it is enough to prove independence. Suppose it contained a circuit of adM, and choose an element C*(er;B) in that circuit. By Proposition 7.3, Cstar[er]={C*∈C(M*): er∉C*} is a hyperplane of adM. This hyperplane contains every fundamental cocircuit C*(ei;B) with i≠r, because er is not in those cocircuits, but it does not contain C*(er;B), because er∈C*(er;B). In a matroid, if all but one element of a circuit lie in a flat, then the remaining element also lies in that flat. This contradiction proves independence, hence the set is a basis.

proof_techniques.name: Relabel an adjoint by hyperplanes and encode elements as incidence hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: A central move is Proposition 3.1: instead of treating the adjoint abstractly, relabel E(adM) by the hyperplanes H(M) of M. Under this labeling, every element e of M determines the subset H[e] of hyperplanes containing e, and the adjoint condition is exactly that each H[e] be a hyperplane of adM. This turns the existence of an order-reversing flat-lattice embedding into a concrete incidence condition between elements and hyperplanes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already has an inclusion of L(M)^op into a larger poset Dr(M). This technique suggests looking for an incidence-theoretic description of the flat stratum F↦Trop(M/F⊕U_{0,F}) and asking whether any candidate involution on Dr(M) is forced by how it acts on the analogues of the sets H[e].

proof_techniques.name: Extension-lattice sandwich for modular matroids
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the extension lattice E(M), whose elements are linear subclasses of hyperplanes, as an ambient lattice for all adjoints of M. Cheung’s characterization gives a sandwich L(M)^op⊆L(adM)⊆E(M). For modular simple M, Lemma 4.2 proves that the natural map X↦{hyperplanes containing X} identifies L(M)^op with E(M), so the sandwich collapses and the adjoint is unique.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If Dr(M) plays a role analogous to an extension or enlargement poset of L(M)^op, the modular case may be rigid: the flat part could already be maximal among adjoint-like structures. This is a possible route to either constructing a unique extension or proving that extra Dressian elements obstruct it.

proof_techniques.name: Use opposite-lattice geometricity to force modularity
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.1 exploits the classical fact that a finite geometric lattice has a geometric opposite exactly in the modular case. Thus an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op exists precisely for modular M. Theorem 4.8 strengthens this in many cases: if M has an adjoint isomorphic to itself and no rank-3 connected components, then M is modular, and conversely modularity gives a self-adjoint matroid.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution of L(M) is a self-duality of the flat lattice, so in the simple finite setting this strongly suggests first reducing to modular/projective-geometric matroids. This can sharply narrow the cases in which an extension to Dr(M) could exist.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce adjoint questions componentwise
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that adjoints preserve and reflect direct-sum decompositions: if M decomposes, its adjoint decomposes into adjoints of the components, and if an adjoint decomposes, then M decomposes accordingly. Hence connectedness is equivalent for M and adM, and adjoint existence is determined by connected components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matroid M in the original problem is disconnected, the flat-lattice involution may permute or reverse components. This technique suggests reducing any extension problem to connected factors and then reassembling, while tracking whether the proposed involution swaps factors.

proof_techniques.name: Fundamental hyperplane and cocircuit bases as tests for adjoint structure
proof_techniques.description: Given a basis B of M, Lemma 3.2 shows that the fundamental hyperplanes H(e;B), for e∈B, form a basis of any hyperplane-labeled adjoint. Dually, Lemma 7.5 shows that the fundamental cocircuits C*(e;B) form a basis in the cocircuit-labeled model. These basis criteria convert abstract adjoint structure into checkable behavior on the fundamental objects associated to a basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For tropical linear spaces inside Trop(M), bases and fundamental circuits/cocircuits control local cones of the Bergman fan. These lemmas suggest checking a candidate Dr(M)-involution on local stars around basis cones or on subspaces associated with fundamental flats.

proof_techniques.name: Projective-geometric duality via normal complements
proof_techniques.description: For connected modular matroids of rank at least four, the paper identifies them with finite projective geometries PG(r−1,q). In that setting, the adjoint can be realized by sending each hyperplane to its one-dimensional normal complement; this gives an isomorphism between PG(r−1,q) and its type I adjoint. Proposition 4.5 similarly identifies the lattice of the dual projective geometry with the opposite lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If M is projective-geometric or realizable with a nondegenerate polarity, this is the closest matroidal analogue of extending F↦F^perp. It suggests trying a tropical orthogonal-complement or polar-dual operation on tropical linear subspaces as the candidate Φ.

proof_techniques.name: Adjoint iteration as a rigidity detector
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.2 embeds M into ad^2 M, Lemma 5.3 reduces any finite adjoint-cycle to period one or two, and Theorem 5.4 shows that cyclic adjoint sequences stabilize at finite projective geometries. Infinite nonrepeating sequences have direct limits that are projective geometries. Thus repeated adjoint-like dualization tends to force projective geometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on Dr(M) would impose a strict period-two symmetry on a much larger poset. The adjoint-sequence results suggest using periodicity as a rigidity or obstruction principle: if the flat part induces adjoint-like cycles, projective geometry may be forced.

proof_techniques.name: Dual cocircuit formulation
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 7.3 rewrites adjoints using cocircuits rather than hyperplanes: relabel the adjoint ground set by C(M*) and require the sets of cocircuits avoiding a fixed element to be hyperplanes. This formulation aligns adjoints with derived-matroid constructions and with the standard circuit-cocircuit duality of matroid theory.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The notation F↦F^perp in the original problem evokes duality. The cocircuit formulation may be more natural than the hyperplane formulation when comparing tropical orthogonality, valuated matroid duality, or tropical linear spaces defined by circuits.

other_useful_info: The paper relies on Cheung [9] and Alfter-Kern-Wanka [2] for the characterization of adjoints via the extension lattice. It uses Greene [15, Theorem 2], which says a simple matroid M is modular if and only if |H(M)|=|E(M)|. It cites Bixby-Coullard [7] for type I adjoints of representable matroids and for the fact used in Theorem 5.9 that adjoints of representable matroids are type I adjoints up to isomorphism. The authors explicitly note that rank 3 is exceptional for self-adjointness because some projective planes are not self-dual, which is why Theorem 4.8 excludes rank-3 connected components. Section 7 connects adjoints to combinatorial derived matroids and records Conjecture 7.2 from Freij-Hollanti-Jurrius-Kuznetsova: if M* has an adjoint, then the combinatorial derived matroid δM should be isomorphic to one of the adjoints of M*. The paper ends with Question 7.8 asking how to construct a combinatorial adjoint for an arbitrary matroid.
why_relevant: Modern treatment of matroid adjoints, opposite lattices, modular matroids, and adjoint sequences. Since an order-reversing involution on flats is a strong adjoint/polarity phenomenon, these results are central.
